input.guest: Dr. Nadia Osman — climate policy researcher
input.raw_transcript:
SPEAKER 1: so um today we're we're talking about something that i think is is really important and uh not enough people understand which is um the difference between you know climate policy and climate politics
SPEAKER 2: yeah so thats thats a great distinction to make and i think uh the the honest answer is that most people dont realize that the policy side has actually moved quite far forward its the politics that that thats stuck
SPEAKER 1: and and and when you say stuck what do you mean specifically like is it is it that that politicians dont understand the science or
SPEAKER 2: no no its not about understanding the science at all i mean most most senior policymakers they they get it they understand the the urgency the problem is incentive structures right so you have a situation where the the costs of climate action are immediate and visible but the benefits are are diffuse and and long term and that creates a a very specific political problem that is different from a policy problem
SPEAKER 1: thats fascinating i hadnt thought about it that way before the the incentive piece

input.guest: Professor Henrik Larsen — EU trade policy analyst based in Brussels
input.raw_transcript:
SPEAKER 1: so professor larson um lets talk about the the new eu uk trade review thats thats coming up because i think a lot of people are confused about what what it actually means
SPEAKER 2: yes so first of all its larsen not larson
SPEAKER 1: oh im so sorry professor larsen
SPEAKER 2: no no thats fine happens all the time so the the trade review is is essentially a a scheduled checkpoint in the trade and cooperation agreement the the tca um and what it means practically is that both sides have an opportunity to to renegotiate certain elements particularly around um sanitary and phytosanitary measures which is which is basically food safety standards and and veterinary checks at the border
SPEAKER 1: and and why does that matter to to ordinary people
SPEAKER 2: well because the the current arrangements are are adding approximately 6 to 8 percent to the cost of of food imports from the eu into the uk and and thats thats being passed directly to consumers in in supermarket prices
input.known_corrections: Larsen (not Larson), TCA (Trade and Cooperation Agreement), SPS (sanitary and phytosanitary)
